title: When Variables Become Functions
content_html:
When Variables Become Functions

this complements [[Self-Mutating Variables]]

This guide explores a powerful evolution in generative art: transforming static variables into dynamic functions. This transformation allows for emergent, time-sensitive, and reactive behavior in creative code, particularly in Processing.

##

## 🔁 Why Turn a Variable into a Function?

float speed = 2.0;

becomes:

float speed() {
  return sin(frameCount * 0.01) * 5;
}
  • The value updates over time
  • The logic becomes portable and composable
  • The system exhibits feedback-like behavior

🎛 Basic Example: Dynamic Radius

float radius() {
  return 100 + sin(frameCount * 0.05) * 20;
}

ellipse(width/2, height/2, radius(), radius());

The radius now oscillates over time.

🧠 With Memory (State Retention)

float base = 100;
float offset = 0;

float radius() {
  offset += noise(frameCount * 0.01) * 0.05;
  return base + sin(offset) * 40;
}

This function accumulates state, evolving non-linearly. ##

## 🔂 Self-Modifying Logic

float step = 0.01;
float offset = 0;

float angle() {
  offset += sin(offset) * 0.005;
  return offset;
}
angle()

modifies its own behavior internally — a form of recursive mutation.

## 📌 **Practical Use Cases**
Static Variable Functional Equivalent Behavior
float x float x() Moves with time, feedback, noise
float angle float angle() Driven by sin, noise, or state
float strokeWeight float strokeW() Reacts to curve or entropy

🧬 Design Implications

* Cleaner abstraction: logic is separated from state

  • Dynamic systems: nothing is fixed, everything reacts
  • Generative depth: even simple sketches become alive
